content: @chunhtai I need to do some minor cleanup + add comments, but if you want to take a look tomorrow and give another review PTAL :)

My understanding from the reviews and an offline discussion earlier was that this is ready to land. There's some cleanup and significant comments here, so I'm not sure I agree anymore.

I'll be OOO starting midday tomorrow, so I will try to address these comments, but will likely not land it. @reidbaker if I get it to a place where I feel confident that the reviews are addressed, I'll let you know in case you can land this while I'm out. Thank you everyone for the feedback and helping push this forward!

Tangential to this pr.  for detecting visibility, looks like we already have this widget https://github.com/google/flutter.widgets/blob/master/packages/visibility_detector/lib/src/render_visibility_detector.dart#L17

> Tangential to this pr. for detecting visibility, looks like we already have this widget https://github.com/google/flutter.widgets/blob/master/packages/visibility_detector/lib/src/render_visibility_detector.dart#L17

Noted this in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/160051
This is ready for another look!

> ### Timeline
> 
> #### Required for feature shipping
> -  Land this PR with the method channel implementation of content sensitivity.
> 
> #### Short-term follow up tasks
> - Ensure every `FlutterView` has a unique ID for the native `SensitiveContentPlugin` to identify them by: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/162685
> - Add `SensitiveContent` widget to relevant Flutter widgets: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/167302
> 
> #### Long-term follow up tasks
> - Convert this PR to use JNIgen: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/160050. Verify no frames are dropped, revealing sensitive content during media projection: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/164820.
> 
> #### Stretch goals
> - Make the `SensitiveContent` widget sensitive to the visibility of child widgets: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/160051
> - Implement `autoSensitivity` mode: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/160879
> - Investigate backwards compatibility for APIs < 35: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/159208

Update: Had an offline @reidbaker and @jmagman, and we have decided that this feature is ok to ship into master without implementing it without JNIgen (ensuring  https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/164820 is fixed), primarily because landing this as-is will only improve things from a security perspective, even with the frame drop possibility. By ship, I mean exposing the `SensitiveContent` widget to users in the widgets library and noting the security vulnerability in the `SensitiveContent` widget documentation in this PR, then landing it. I'll follow up with these changes shortly.

After this lands, I'll work on the short term tasks listed above, including implementing relevant Flutter widgets with `SensitiveContent`.

In the long term, I'll plan to return to the JNIgen work, though this would have to follow the architectural work of determining how we (Flutter) plans for JNI/FFI gen to work in the embedder for all platforms. This will require significant time and effort to do it right, which was a significant factor for making the decision to ship this feature as-is. When the work of determining how to use JNI/FFI gen in the embedder is picked up, the sensitive content feature will be a concrete example that can be used to demonstrate a proof of concept.

>  Lets assume that we add this widget to a password field in the material library. How does a user turn off sensitive content for an integration test they have already built after updating to this version of flutter and the new version of material.

I assume we would add a field to `TextField` that would act as a switch for setting the sensitivity of the wrapper `SensitiveContent` widget in its implementation. By default, we can set it to `notSensitive` to not break people. Even if we don't, though, they can then just use the field to `notSensitive` to fix any other breakages.

> > Lets assume that we add this widget to a password field in the material library. How does a user turn off sensitive content for an integration test they have already built after updating to this version of flutter and the new version of material.
> 
> I assume we would add a field to `TextField` that would act as a switch for setting the sensitivity of the wrapper `SensitiveContent` widget in its implementation. By default, we can set it to `notSensitive` to not break people. Even if we don't, though, they can then just use the field to `notSensitive` to fix any other breakages.

That would mean that in test runs they would need to build a way to override the value.

This value is sensitive and they want to protect their users but they dont want the protection during debug mode during testing. 

I think this user journey, the one where you want it off during testing is the one that I am the most worried about.

If the `TextField` is baked deep into their implementation, then yes, they would need to expose a way to override the field we add from a test. Maybe I have to think deeper, but I think that's unavoidable; the only other alternative is to not bake it into Flutter widgets like `TextField` to begin with.

I think it's fair to ask developers to build that functionality, and I don't think that making them do so is a good enough reason to decide against baking `SensitiveContent` into relevant Flutter widgets. Building that functionality is likely not super difficult and certainly wouldn't outweigh the added security benefits.

> I think I caught a place where errors can be silently swallowed, see comment below.
> 
> About the integration test discussion [above](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/158473#pullrequestreview-2776484926), does that situation only apply to integration tests that are actually testing sharing their screen to another device?

Yes it only applies to integration tests where the output of the screen is streamed.

> Would it be possible to mock isSupported and fake that sensitive content is not supported at all? It's easy to do this in a unit test ([example](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/457e98a1e70b74b27a209721484c28e73a80caef/packages/flutter/test/services/text_input_test.dart#L232-L236)) but I'm not sure about in an integration test.

That's what I'm thinking! For integration tests, developers would just need to expose a way to change their widget in an integration test to turn sensitive content sensitivity off.
My force push was after running `git rebase --onto main HEAD`

## Screen flickering on iOS since Flutter 2.10.0

App screen flickers and freezes, when I restart it, it goes away. This happened after upgrading to flutter 2.10.0, it doesn't always happen.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19810700/159396407-ea553089-e4c3-4f0e-b709-d169cbefed7f.MOV

Hi @sy5075391, can you upgrade to stable 2.10.3 to see if you still experience this issue? 

If you do, please provide the updated `flutter doctor -v` as well as a complete minimal reproducible code sample that we can use to replicate this. 

Thank you

@sy5075391 If you have a reproducible example of this, I would love to take a look at it. We have a handful of reports of a similar flickering in our production app. Unfortunately, I haven't been able to reproduce it myself, and we've only gotten like 4 reports over the course of 2 months. Still, it would be nice to figure out. Here's what it looks like for us (very similar–I added the black box just to obscure personal details from the user):

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3249979/159985572-3e3dbb2f-e752-4e4a-8987-c5f95e93ddf9.mp4

We're still on a 2.10.2-based version, so I'll see about updating to 2.10.3 as well (we have a few extra crash fixes and undo support built in). This PR in 2.10.3 seems like the most likely thing to be relevant: https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/31656

We've only seen this on iOS (15.2.1 and 15.3.1), but not iPadOS.

I have experienced the same problem (both IOS and Android) in two of my apps since the upgrade from 2.8 to 2.10.  But the issue happens randomly. so far I cannot isolate and replicate the bug. It happens when the screen either has a lot of images or playing video.

If anyone else is experiencing this issue, kindly upvote the initial comment on this issue. We would also appreciate it if any 
of you experiencing this issue can provide a sample so that we can verify this. 

Keeping this issue open to track future reports of the same issue. 

Based on what I've gathered: 

- This issue is on both android and iOS https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/100522#issuecomment-1096446674
- It doesn't seem to affect platform views https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/100522#issuecomment-1090079625
- Its occurrence is sporadic and there isn't a clear way to isolate the issue
- All reports seem to indicate this happening after upgrading to flutter 2.10

Once again, if anyone is able to reproduce and isolate the cause, kindly provide a sample so that we can investigate this.

I looks like it might be related to partial repaint (one of the back buffers seem to be having invalid content), but it's also quite strange that most of these videos are showing static scene without any animation and yet the screen keeps repainting. 

A reproducible example would be great to have.

Also, if it happens, does it get fixed by scrolling (or similar action that triggers repaint of large portion of screen)?

I use flutter 2.10.5

This doesn't seem to be related to partial repaint, at least not directly. But if even one frame fails to rasterize like this it will cause flicker when flipping buffers since one of the buffers will have stale data.

when flickering, there is some logs maybe can help deal with this problem.

@knopp Is there a more graceful way to recover if a frame fails to render?
I'm not sure, but why does it matter? Are these errors to be expected?

I agree with @knopp that the errors are not expected. We recently wired up enhanced command buffer debugger in [a different renderer](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/cafc4eb02659e3cf5f6b695ecc8ae820fdcc0cee/impeller/renderer/backend/metal/command_buffer_mtl.mm#L79). I wonder if the Skia renderer command buffers have similar instrumentation in place. Usually, by the time you see "for causing prior/excessive GPU errors" in the logs the instigating failure has happened a long time ago and we need the first instance of the failure.
@knopp What made use suspect it was partial repaint was that this happening on both iOS and Android. But it could totally be something rendering related on all backends.
Some clients also reported this in our production app, but we couldn't reproduce it locally. Until now, we only got occurrences from iOS.

Are any of these issues happening only on an iPhone 13 (with ProMotion displays) and/or with a platform view in the scene? We still need additional information about how and when this happens. The following would be good to know:

1. Does this happen on iOS or Android or both?
2. Does the application have a platform view in the scene when this happens?
3. Does this happen on devices with higher refresh rates?

In my case, it only happened on the iPhone. This has happened not only in debugging but also in released apps. My iPhone model is iPhone XS MAX and it occurs from the flutter version 2.10.0 or higher. It always happens suddenly when the iPhone home bar is swiped upward. I'm sorry, but I can't provide you with the video. It also occurs too in static app (pages), regardless of whether the app is refreshed or not.
We just got one report about this, on an iPhone 12 mini. The screen didn't have a platform view.

I’m also facing this issue randomly. We’re still on the beta-testing phase but this only happens on iOS. We had reports happening on iPhone 12 so no ProMotion. 

I’m not able to reproduce it but it seems it starts when trying to exit the app when dragging the white bar. 

When the app enters on this flickering state it’s unresponsive and you have to force quit.

If you mean native widgets, no not that we're aware of.
